content.full_text:
January 18, 1991

Mr. Scott Hallabrin  
Acting General Counsel  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
428 J Street, Suite 800  
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Scott:

Attached is a letter seeking confirmation of the recent advice you rendered about the date a contribution is "made".

As we discussed, the FPPC's interpretation of when a contribution is "made" impacts the regulated community as a whole—not only major donors who make "late" or other monetary contributions. Presumably, the rule stated in my confirmation letter (if confirmed) would apply in determining when a contribution is "made" for purposes of compliance with prohibitions on "making" excessive contributions in special elections (Gov. Code § 85305, Ch. 5) and for determining when a lobbyist-employer or lobbying firm must include a contribution "made" in a particular quarter on its report for that quarter. (Gov Code § 86114, 86116, Ch. 6. See also Gov. Code § 84308(d), and Gov. Code § 15625(e).)

Recipients of contributions also have various similar reporting obligations and prohibitions with which they must comply. As you know, in August, 1990, this firm requested reconsideration of advice given on the "date of receipt" issue where a contribution is delivered to an intermediary for a sponsored political action committee.

As we discussed, the Fair Political Practices Commission, the office of the Secretary of State,1 and various other enforcers and filing officers may penalize filers who do not comply with requirements to file timely contribution reports, or who fail to return excessive or potentially disqualifying contributions timely. (See, e.g., Gov. Code § 84203.) But "timeliness" depends on when the contribution is "made" or "received." There is currently no FPPC regulation which

1 We understand the office of the Secretary of State has been informed of the two standards discussed above regarding when a contribution is "made."

Mr. Scott Hallabrin  
January 18, 1991  
Page 2

clarifies the date when making and receipt occurs. You indicated in our conversation that a regulation might be helpful. We concur. The present off-election year could be an opportune time to address clarification of these fundamental concepts.

Thank you again for your courtesy and helpfulness.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
JUDITH SPROUL DAVIS

JSD:kab  
6278.03

cc: Deona Vastine, Office of the Secretary of State  
    Gregory W. Baugher
